title_en: Comprehensive Discourse on the Essentials of Truth
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: 'I already understand how the five qi intermingle and how fullness and depletion alternately arise. But as for the six qi governing in their respective divisions, presiding over heaven and earth, how do they arrive?' Qibo bowed twice and replied: 'How penetrating this question is! This is the great regulating principle of heaven and earth, and it corresponds throughout to the human spirit.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear how above it accords with the bright and manifest, and below it accords with the dark and hidden.' Qibo said: 'This is what the Way governs, yet it is what practitioners find perplexing.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear this Way.' Qibo said: 'When reverting yin governs heaven, its transformation is by wind; when lesser yin governs heaven, its transformation is by heat; when greater yin governs heaven, its transformation is by dampness; when lesser yang governs heaven, its transformation is by fire; when yang brightness governs heaven, its transformation is by dryness; when greater yang governs heaven, its transformation is by cold. According to the visceral position over which each presides, one names the disease that arises.' The Emperor said: 'What of the transformations of the earth?' Qibo said: 'They correspond in the same manner as those governing heaven; the intervening qi are likewise so.' The Emperor said: 'What is meant by the intervening qi?' Qibo said: 'Those that govern the left and right positions are called the intervening qi.' The Emperor said: 'How do they differ?' Qibo said: 'That which governs the year regulates the year as a whole; the intervening qi regulate the successive steps within it.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of the governance of the year?' Qibo said: 'When reverting yin governs heaven, its transformation is wind; when it is at the spring, its transformation is sour; when it governs the qi of the year, its transformation is verdant; as an intervening qi, its transformation is movement. When lesser yin governs heaven, its transformation is heat; when it is at the spring, its transformation is bitter; it does not govern the transformation of the year as a whole; when it resides as an intervening qi, its transformation is scorching. When greater yin governs heaven, its transformation is dampness; when it is at the spring, its transformation is sweet; when it governs the qi, its transformation is yellow; as an intervening qi, its transformation is softening. When lesser yang governs heaven, its transformation is fire; when it is at the spring, its transformation is bitter; when it governs the qi, its transformation is scarlet; as an intervening qi, its transformation is brightness. When yang brightness governs heaven, its transformation is dryness; when it is at the spring, its transformation is acrid; when it governs the qi, its transformation is white; as an intervening qi, its transformation is clearing. When greater yang governs heaven, its transformation is cold; when it is at the spring, its transformation is salty; when it governs the qi, its transformation is black; as an intervening qi, its transformation is storing. Therefore, one who treats disease must clearly understand how the six transformations govern their respective divisions, what is generated by the five flavors and five colors, and what is suitable for the five viscera; only then can one speak of the threads by which fullness, depletion, and the generation of disease arise.'

The Emperor said: 'That reverting yin at the spring transforms into sourness, this I already know. But how does the transformation of wind proceed?' Qibo said: 'Wind circulates over the earth; this is what is called the root. The remaining qi follow the same pattern. That which is rooted in heaven is the qi of heaven; that which is rooted in earth is the qi of earth. When the qi of heaven and earth combine, the six divisions are apportioned and the myriad things are transformed and generated. Hence it is said: carefully observe what is appropriate to the qi, and do not lose the pivotal mechanism of disease, this is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'How does it govern disease?' Qibo said: 'If one prepares materia medica according to what governs the year, then nothing essential to its governance will be lost.' The Emperor said: 'What are the substances proper to the year?' Qibo said: 'They are the concentrated essence of heaven and earth.' The Emperor said: 'What of those that govern the intervening qi?' Qibo said: 'Those that govern the qi are the same as those governing the year, yet there is surplus and insufficiency in their potency.' The Emperor said: 'What is meant by substances that do not govern the year?' Qibo said: 'They are dispersed in potency. Hence though their substance is the same, their grade differs: their qi and flavor may be thin or thick, their nature and function may be hasty or calm, their curative and preservative power may be greater or lesser, and the depth of their transformative force may be shallow or deep, this is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'What is meant by the year's governance bringing harm to a viscus?' Qibo said: 'One names it according to that which it cannot overcome, this is the essential point.' The Emperor said: 'How does one treat it?' Qibo said: 'When excess above overflows below, one levels it with that which overcomes it; when excess outside overflows within, one treats it with that which overcomes it.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of leveling the qi?' Qibo said: 'Carefully examine where yin and yang reside and regulate accordingly, taking balance as the goal. What is straightforward is treated by direct treatment; what is contrary is treated by contrary treatment.'

The Emperor said: 'You, Master, have said that one examines where yin and yang reside in order to regulate it. The classic text states that when the Man Ying and Cun Kou pulses correspond to one another, as though a cord were drawn taut, equal in size at both ends, this is called balance. What then of where yin resides at the Cun Kou?' Qibo said: 'Observe whether the year is of southern or northern governance, and it can be known.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear the whole of it.' Qibo said: 'In years of northern governance, if lesser yin is at the spring, then the Cun Kou does not respond; if reverting yin is at the spring, the right does not respond; if greater yin is at the spring, the left does not respond. In years of southern governance, if lesser yin governs heaven, then the Cun Kou does not respond; if reverting yin governs heaven, the right does not respond; if greater yin governs heaven, the left does not respond. In all cases of non-response, if one reverses the side of examination, the pulse will appear.' The Emperor said: 'What of the sign at the Chi position?' Qibo said: 'In years of northern governance, if the three yin are below, the Cun does not respond; if the three yin are above, the Chi does not respond. In years of southern governance, if the three yin are in heaven, the Cun does not respond; if the three yin are at the spring, the Chi does not respond, and this holds the same for the left and right sides. Hence it is said: he who knows the essential can finish it in a single phrase; he who does not know the essential is scattered and lost without end, this is what is meant.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. When the qi of heaven and earth overflow within and cause disease, how is it?' Qibo said: 'In a year when reverting yin is at the spring, wind excess prevails: the qi of the earth is obscured, the level plains grow dim, and the grasses flourish prematurely. People suffer from shivering chills, frequent stretching and yawning, heart pain with distension to the sides, urgent tightness in both flanks, inability to take in food and drink, blockage of the diaphragm and throat, vomiting after eating, abdominal distension with frequent belching; when they obtain a bowel movement or pass qi, they feel eased as though the affliction had abated, though the whole body remains heavy. In a year when lesser yin is at the spring, heat excess prevails: flames seem to float over rivers and marshes, and shaded places appear bright. People suffer from constant rumbling in the abdomen, qi surging up to the chest, panting such that they cannot stand for long, alternating cold and heat with pain in the skin, dimness of vision, toothache, swelling of the cheeks, aversion to cold and fever resembling malaria, pain and distension in the lower abdomen; hibernating creatures fail to go into hiding. In a year when greater yin is at the spring, the grasses flourish prematurely, and dampness excess prevails: dust and haze obscure the crags and valleys, and yellow tones appear black instead. At the confluence of utmost yin, people suffer from fluid accumulation with heart pain, deafness, a dull and muddled sensation, swelling of the throat with throat-block, bleeding disorders of the lower yin regions, painful swelling of the lower abdomen with inability to urinate, disease with surging headache such that the eyes feel as if bulging out, the nape as if being pulled, the lower back as if broken, the thighs unable to turn, the popliteal fossa as if knotted, and the calves as if split apart. In a year when lesser yang is at the spring, fire excess prevails: flames blaze brightly over the outlying fields, and cold and heat arrive in alternation. People suffer from pouring diarrhea with red and white discharge, pain in the lower abdomen with reddish urine, and in severe cases blood in the stool, corresponding in the same manner as lesser yin. In a year when yang brightness is at the spring, dryness excess prevails: fog and mist bring clear darkness. People are prone to retching, vomiting with a bitter taste, frequent sighing, pain in the heart and flanks such that they cannot turn from side to side, and in severe cases dryness of the throat, a dusty complexion, a body lacking luster, with the outer aspect of the feet turning hot instead. In a year when greater yang is at the spring, cold excess prevails: there is a congealing, stern, bleak severity. People suffer from lower abdominal pain drawing upon the testicles, pulling on the lumbar spine and surging up to cause heart pain, with bleeding, sore throat, and swelling of the jaw.' The Emperor said: 'Good. How does one treat these?' Qibo said: 'For all the qi at the spring: when wind is in excess within, treat with acrid and cool substances, assisted by bitter ones; moderate it with sweetness and disperse it with acridity. When heat is in excess within, treat with salty and cold substances, assisted by sweet and bitter ones; astringe it with sourness and vent it with bitterness. When dampness is in excess within, treat with bitter and hot substances, assisted by sour and bland ones; dry it with bitterness and drain it with blandness. When fire is in excess within, treat with salty and cold substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones; astringe it with sourness and vent it with bitterness. When dryness is in excess within, treat with bitter and warm substances, assisted by sweet and acrid ones; purge it with bitterness. When cold is in excess within, treat with sweet and hot substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones; drain it with saltiness, moisten it with acridity, and firm it with bitterness.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What of the changes wrought by the qi of heaven?' Qibo said: 'When reverting yin governs heaven, wind excess prevails: the great void is dusty and dim, clouds and vapors are disturbed, cold arises within the spring qi, and flowing water fails to freeze. People suffer from pain in the stomach duct opposite the heart, extending upward to the two flanks, blockage of the diaphragm and throat, inability to take in food and drink, stiffness at the root of the tongue, vomiting after eating, diarrhea with cold, abdominal distension, loose stools, abdominal concretions, and retention of water; hibernating creatures fail to depart their burrows. The disease is rooted in the spleen. If the Chongyang pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. When lesser yin governs heaven, heat excess prevails: oppressive heat arrives and fire carries out its governance. People suffer from vexing heat in the chest, dryness of the throat, fullness of the right side, pain in the skin, alternating cold and heat with cough and panting; heavy rains are about to arrive; there is spitting of blood and bleeding from below, nosebleed, sneezing, and retching, and change in the color of the urine; in severe cases, sores and swelling, pain in the shoulders, back, upper arms, and supraclavicular fossa, heart pain and lung distension, great abdominal fullness and bloating, bloating with panting and cough. The disease is rooted in the lung. If the Chize pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. When greater yin governs heaven, dampness excess prevails: heavy overcast clouds spread across the sky, and rain transforms what is withered. There is swelling, bone pain, and yin blockage, in yin blockage, pressing on the affected area yields nothing; pain in the lower back, spine, head, and nape with intermittent dizziness; difficulty with bowel movement; the yin qi fails to function; hunger without desire to eat; coughing up blood-streaked sputum; a sensation as though the heart were suspended. The disease is rooted in the kidney. If the Taixi pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. When lesser yang governs heaven, fire excess prevails: warm qi circulates freely, and the governance of metal is disrupted. People suffer from headache, fever, and aversion to cold resembling malaria; heat rising with pain in the skin; the complexion turning yellow and red; the condition transmitting to become edema, with swelling of the body and face; abdominal fullness with labored breathing requiring the head tilted back; pouring diarrhea with red and white discharge; sores and coughing up of blood; vexation of the heart with heat in the chest; and in severe cases, nosebleed. The disease is rooted in the lung. If the Tianfu pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. When yang brightness governs heaven, dryness excess prevails: trees flourish late, grasses grow late, and there are internal changes in the sinews and bones. People suffer from pain in the left flank, cold settling within the body giving rise to malaria-like conditions, great coolness that transforms the season's normal signs, coughing, rumbling in the abdomen with pouring diarrhea and duck-slop stools. Renowned trees contract and their growth withers below, while the tips of grasses scorch above. There is sudden severe pain in the heart and flanks such that one cannot turn from side to side, dryness of the throat, a dusty complexion, lower back pain, hernial swelling in men, lower abdominal pain in women, dimness at the corners of the eyes, sores, papules, and carbuncles; hibernating creatures come out of hiding prematurely. The disease is rooted in the liver. If the Taichong pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. When greater yang governs heaven, cold excess prevails: cold qi arrives contrary to season, water freezes, and blood is altered within, breaking out as sores and abscesses. People suffer from reversal heart pain, vomiting of blood, bleeding from below, nosebleed, frequent sorrow, intermittent dizziness and collapse; when the fire movement blazes fiercely, sudden rain turns to hail. There is fullness of chest and abdomen, heat in the hands, spasm of the elbows, and a surging sensation in the axilla; the heart pounds and races violently; the chest, flanks, and stomach duct feel unsettled; the face is red and the eyes yellow; frequent belching and dry throat; in severe cases the complexion turns sooty black, with thirst and desire to drink. The disease is rooted in the heart. If the Shenmen pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. This is what is meant by knowing the affected viscus through the pulsation of its qi.' The Emperor said: 'Good. How does one treat these?' Qibo said: 'For the qi governing heaven: when wind excess prevails, level it with acrid and cool substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones; moderate it with sweetness and drain it with sourness. When heat excess prevails, level it with salty and cold substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones; astringe it with sourness. When dampness excess prevails, level it with bitter and hot substances, assisted by sour and acrid ones; dry it with bitterness and drain it with blandness. If dampness above is severe and accompanied by heat, treat with bitter and warm substances, assisted by sweet and acrid ones, and stop once sweating has been induced. When fire excess prevails, level it with sour and cold substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones; astringe it with sourness, vent it with bitterness, and restore it with sourness, the same applies to heat excess. When dryness excess prevails, level it with bitter and moistening substances, assisted by sour and acrid ones; purge it with bitterness. When cold excess prevails, level it with acrid and hot substances, assisted by sweet and bitter ones; drain it with saltiness.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. When the pathogenic qi is instead overcome by that which should not naturally prevail over it, how does one treat this?' Qibo said: 'When wind governs at the earth but clearness instead prevails over it, treat with sour and warm substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones, and level it with acridity. When heat governs at the earth but cold instead prevails over it, treat with sweet and hot substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones, and level it with saltiness. When dampness governs at the earth but heat instead prevails over it, treat with bitter and cold substances, assisted by salty and sweet ones, and level it with bitterness. When fire governs at the earth but cold instead prevails over it, treat with sweet and hot substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones, and level it with saltiness. When dryness governs at the earth but heat instead prevails over it, treat with mild and cold substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones, level it with sourness, and take harmony as the beneficial course. When cold governs at the earth but heat instead prevails over it, treat with salty and cold substances, assisted by sweet and acrid ones, and level it with bitterness.' The Emperor said: 'What of when the pathogenic qi prevails over what governs heaven?' Qibo said: 'When wind transforms at heaven but clearness instead prevails over it, treat with sour and warm substances, assisted by sweet and bitter ones. When heat transforms at heaven but cold instead prevails over it, treat with sweet and warm substances, assisted by bitter, sour, and acrid ones. When dampness transforms at heaven but heat instead prevails over it, treat with bitter and cold substances, assisted by bitter and sour ones. When fire transforms at heaven but cold instead prevails over it, treat with sweet and hot substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones. When dryness transforms at heaven but heat instead prevails over it, treat with acrid and cold substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones. When cold transforms at heaven but heat instead prevails over it, treat with salty and cold substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones.'

The Emperor said: 'How do the six qi mutually dominate one another?' Qibo said: 'When reverting yin dominates: ringing in the ears, dizziness of the head, a muddled sensation with desire to vomit, a chilled feeling in the stomach and diaphragm; great winds rise repeatedly, and bare-skinned creatures fail to reproduce; qi gathers in the sides and flanks and transforms into heat; the urine turns yellow-red; pain in the stomach duct opposite the heart extending up to both flanks; intestinal rumbling with undigested-food diarrhea; pain in the lower abdomen; pouring discharge of red and white; in severe cases, vomiting, with blockage of the diaphragm and throat. When lesser yin dominates: heat below the heart with frequent hunger; throbbing below the navel; qi roams through the triple burner; blazing summer heat arrives; trees exude sap and grasses wilt; retching and vomiting with agitation and vexation; abdominal fullness and pain with loose stools, transmitting to become a reddish discharge. When greater yin dominates: fire qi is stifled within, sores erupt within and spread scattered without; the disease lies in the flanks; in severe cases, heart pain with heat blockage, headache, throat-block, and stiffness of the nape. When dampness dominates alone, damp qi is stifled within, cold presses upon the lower burner, pain lodges in the vertex and pulls mutually between the eyebrows; fullness of the stomach; rain falls repeatedly and, paradoxically, signs of dryness appear; fullness and heavy stiffness of the lower abdomen and buttocks; internal discomfort with frequent pouring diarrhea; warmth beneath the feet; heaviness of the head, swelling of the lower legs and feet; fluid accumulation arises within, and swelling appears above. When lesser yang dominates: heat lodges in the stomach; vexation of the heart with heart pain; redness of the eyes with desire to retch; vomiting of sour fluid with frequent hunger; ear pain, reddish urine; frequent fright, delirious speech; sudden heat that wastes and scorches; grasses wilt and waters dry up; shelled creatures shrink back; pain in the lower abdomen with discharge of red and white below. When yang brightness dominates: clearness arises within; pain in the left flank with loose stools; within, blockage of the throat; without, hernial swelling breaks out; great coolness brings a stern, killing severity; flowers and blossoms change their appearance; furred creatures suffer calamity; discomfort in the chest; throat blockage with cough. When greater yang dominates: a congealing chill arrives, and waters freeze out of season; feathered creatures delay their breeding; hemorrhoids and malarial disorders break out; cold reversal enters the stomach, generating heart pain within; sores form in the yin region, and the private functions become inhibited, pulling mutually into the inner thighs; the sinews and flesh become cramped and stiff; the blood vessels congeal and stagnate; the network vessels become full and the complexion changes; there may be bleeding from below; the skin becomes blocked and swollen; abdominal fullness with reduced appetite; heat instead rises upward, causing pain in the head, nape, fontanelle, vertex, and occiput, with the eyes feeling as if bulging out; cold enters the lower burner and transmits to become watery diarrhea.' The Emperor said: 'How does one treat these?' Qibo said: 'For dominance of reverting yin, treat with sweet and clearing substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones, and drain it with sourness. For dominance of lesser yin, treat with acrid and cold substances, assisted by bitter and salty ones, and drain it with sweetness. For dominance of greater yin, treat with salty and hot substances, assisted by acrid and sweet ones, and drain it with bitterness. For dominance of lesser yang, treat with acrid and cold substances, assisted by sweet and salty ones, and drain it with sweetness. For dominance of yang brightness, treat with sour and warm substances, assisted by acrid and sweet ones, and drain it with bitterness. For dominance of greater yang, treat with sweet and hot substances, assisted by acrid and sour ones, and drain it with saltiness.'

The Emperor said: 'What of the return of the six qi?' Qibo said: 'How thorough is this question! In the return of reverting yin: hardness and fullness of the lower abdomen, urgent tension with sudden pain; trees are flattened and sand flies; bare-skinned creatures fail to flourish; reversal heart pain; sweating with retching and vomiting; food and drink cannot be taken in, or if taken in are brought back up again; trembling and dizziness of sinews and bones; cold reversal; in severe cases it enters the spleen, causing food blockage with vomiting. If the Chongyang pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. In the return of lesser yin: warm heat arises within; vexation and agitation with sneezing and nasal congestion; griping pain of the lower abdomen; fire appears, scorching and burning, with dryness of the throat; discharges that alternate between occurring and stopping; qi stirs on the left and moves up to the right; cough, pain of the skin; sudden loss of voice, heart pain, clouding of the head with loss of consciousness, then shivering aversion to cold, trembling, and delirious speech; cold subsides and heat follows; thirst with desire to drink; shortness of breath, wasting and weakness of the bones, obstruction of the intestines with discomfort; externally, floating edema, hiccup, and belching; the fire qi transforms afterward; flowing water fails to freeze; heat qi runs rampant; shelled creatures fail to reproduce; there arise skin eruptions, papules, sores, carbuncles, deep-rooted abscesses, boils and hemorrhoids; in severe cases it enters the lung, causing cough with a foul nasal discharge. If the Tianfu pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. In the return of greater yin: transformations of dampness arise; heaviness of the body with fullness at the center; food and drink fail to be digested; yin qi reverses upward; discomfort in the chest; fluid accumulation arises within; cough and panting with audible sound; heavy rains fall repeatedly, and scaled creatures appear on dry land; pain and heaviness of the vertex, with trembling and convulsion especially severe; retching with a silent, withdrawn manner, spitting up clear fluid; in severe cases it enters the kidney, causing unrestrained discharge from the orifices. If the Taixi pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. In the return of lesser yang: great heat is about to arrive; withering dryness and scorching burning; shelled creatures waste away; fright and convulsion with cough and nosebleed; heat in the heart with vexation and agitation; frequent bowel movements with aversion to wind; reversal qi moves upward; the face appears as though covered with floating dust; the eyes twitch and convulse; fire qi erupts within, rising above to cause erosive sores of the mouth and retching; overflow bleeding and bleeding from below; breaking out as malarial disorder, with aversion to cold, chattering teeth and trembling; when the cold reaches its extreme it turns instead to heat; the throat and its network vessels become scorched and parched; thirst with craving for fluids; the complexion turns yellow-red; shortness of breath with a wilted pulse; transforming into edema, transmitting to become swelling; in severe cases it enters the lung, causing cough with bleeding from below. If the Chize pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. In the return of yang brightness: clear qi rises in force; dense forests turn ashen and parched; furred creatures suffer pestilence; disease arises in the flank; qi gathers on the left; frequent sighing; in severe cases, heart pain with glomus and fullness; abdominal distension with diarrhea; vomiting of bitter fluid, cough, hiccup, and vexation of the heart; disease located in the diaphragm with headache; in severe cases it enters the liver, causing fright and convulsion of the sinews. If the Taichong pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated. In the return of greater yang: reversal qi moves upward; water congeals and rain turns to ice; feathered creatures perish; cold is generated in the heart and stomach; discomfort in the chest and diaphragm; heart pain with glomus and fullness; headache with frequent sorrow; intermittent dizziness and collapse; reduced appetite with pain instead in the lower back and buttocks; difficulty bending and stretching; the earth cracks and ice hardens; the light of the sun fails to prevail; the lower abdomen draws upon the testicles, pulling on the lumbar spine and surging up toward the heart; spitting up of clear fluid, and hiccup and belching; in severe cases it enters the heart, causing frequent forgetfulness and frequent sorrow. If the Shenmen pulse is cut off, death, and it cannot be treated.' The Emperor said: 'Good. How does one treat these?' Qibo said: 'For the return of reverting yin, treat with sour and cold substances, assisted by sweet and acrid ones; drain it with sourness and moderate it with sweetness. For the return of lesser yin, treat with salty and cold substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones; drain it with sweetness, astringe it with sourness, vent it with acridity and bitterness, and soften it with saltiness. For the return of greater yin, treat with bitter and hot substances, assisted by sour and acrid ones; drain it with bitterness, dry it, and drain it further. For the return of lesser yang, treat with salty and cold substances, assisted by bitter and acrid ones; soften it with saltiness, astringe it with sourness, and vent it with acridity and bitterness. In venting, do not shy from heat, yet do not transgress against warmth or coolness, the same method applies as for lesser yin. For the return of yang brightness, treat with acrid and warm substances, assisted by bitter and sweet ones; drain it with bitterness, purge it with bitterness, and supplement it with sourness. For the return of greater yang, treat with salty and hot substances, assisted by sweet and acrid ones, and firm it with bitterness. In treating all cases of dominance and return: what is cold, heat it; what is hot, cool it; what is warm, clear it; what is cool, warm it; what is dispersed, gather it; what is repressed, disperse it; what is dry, moisten it; what is urgent, moderate it; what is hard, soften it; what is brittle, firm it; what is weakened, supplement it; what is strong, drain it, settling each in its proper qi. One must achieve clarity and stillness, and then the pathogenic qi will decline and depart, returning to its proper source. This is the general framework of treatment.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What is meant by the upper and lower divisions of qi?' Qibo said: 'Above the midpoint of the body, there are three qi, this is the division of heaven, governed by the qi of heaven. Below the midpoint of the body, there are three qi, this is the division of earth, governed by the qi of earth. One names the qi according to its position, and names the location according to the qi, in order to speak of the disease, the midpoint is what is called the Celestial Pivot. Hence, when the upper qi dominates and both above and below fall ill, one names it after the earth division; when the lower qi dominates and both above and below fall ill, one names it after the heaven division. What is called the arrival of the dominant qi is when the answering qi is bent and suppressed, not yet issued forth; once the return arrives, one no longer distinguishes it by the names of heaven and earth, all are treated according to the pattern of the returning qi.'

The Emperor said: 'In the movement of dominance and return, is there a constant timing? Is there a certainty to the qi?' Qibo said: 'The timing has a constant position, but the qi has no certainty.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear this principle.' Qibo said: 'From the first qi through the third qi, the qi of heaven governs, this is the constant period of dominance. From the fourth qi through the final qi, the qi of earth governs, this is the constant period of return. Where there is dominance, there is return; where there is no dominance, there is none.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of when, after the return has ended, dominance occurs again?' Qibo said: 'When dominance arrives, return follows, there is no fixed number of occurrences; it ceases only when it has waned. If, after the return has ended, dominance occurs again and is not met with a further return, harm results, this injures life.' The Emperor said: 'Why does the return itself sometimes cause disease?' Qibo said: 'It resides in a position not its own, and the two fail to accord with each other. When the return greatly overcomes the dominant qi, the governing qi that ought to prevail seasonally instead is overcome by it, hence disease results contrarily. This is what is meant by fire, dryness, and heat arising out of due season.' The Emperor said: 'How does one treat this?' Qibo said: 'As for the dominance of qi: when slight, follow along with it; when severe, control it. As for the return of qi: when mild, level it; when violent, seize control of it. In all cases, follow the dominant qi and settle what is bent and suppressed, without concern for its number of occurrences, taking balance as the goal, this is the principle.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What of the dominance and return between guest and host qi?' Qibo said: 'Between the guest and host qi, there is dominance but no return.' The Emperor said: 'What of their contrariety and compliance?' Qibo said: 'When the host dominates, it is contrary; when the guest dominates, it is compliant, this is the Way of heaven.' The Emperor said: 'How do they generate disease?' Qibo said: 'When reverting yin governs heaven: if the guest dominates, there is ringing in the ears with trembling dizziness, and in severe cases cough; if the host dominates, there is pain in the chest and flanks, and difficulty speaking. When lesser yin governs heaven: if the guest dominates, there is nasal congestion and sneezing, stiffness of the neck and nape, dizzying heat in the shoulders and back, headache, shortness of breath, fever, deafness, and dim vision, and in severe cases swelling with overflow bleeding, sores, cough and panting; if the host dominates, there is heat in the heart with vexation and agitation, and in severe cases pain in the flanks with distension. When greater yin governs heaven: if the guest dominates, there is swelling of the head and face with panting breath; if the host dominates, there is fullness of chest and abdomen with dizziness after eating. When lesser yang governs heaven: if the guest dominates, there break out red papules externally, and also erysipelas-like sores, retching, throat-block, headache, and swollen throat, deafness and overflow bleeding, and internally convulsions; if the host dominates, there is fullness of the chest with cough requiring the head tilted back, and in severe cases bleeding, with heat in the hands. When yang brightness governs heaven, if clearness returns and there is a surplus within, there is cough with nosebleed and blocked throat, heat in the heart and diaphragm; if the cough does not cease and pale blood is coughed up, death results. When greater yang governs heaven: if the guest dominates, there is discomfort in the chest with clear nasal discharge, and cough upon exposure to cold; if the host dominates, there is a rattling sound in the throat. When reverting yin is at the spring: if the guest dominates, the major joints are impaired, with internal spasm, stiffness, and convulsion, and external difficulty of movement; if the host dominates, the sinews and bones shake together, with intermittent pain in the lower back and abdomen. When lesser yin is at the spring: if the guest dominates, there is pain in the lower back, buttocks, thighs, knees, calves, shins, and feet, with dizzying heat and soreness, swelling such that one cannot stand for long, and change in urination and defecation; if the host dominates, reversal qi moves upward, with heart pain and fever, and all manner of blockage arise in the diaphragm, breaking out in the flanks with unrestrained sweating, and cold reversal of the four limbs sets in. When greater yin is at the spring: if the guest dominates, there is wilting weakness of the feet with heaviness below, irregular urination and defecation, dampness lodging in the lower burner, breaking out as watery diarrhea, and swelling with disorders of the private parts; if the host dominates, cold qi reverses and causes fullness, food and drink cannot descend, and in severe cases hernial disorders occur. When lesser yang is at the spring: if the guest dominates, there is pain in the lower back and abdomen with, paradoxically, aversion to cold, and in severe cases white discharge below and turbid urine; if the host dominates, heat instead rises upward and lodges in the heart, causing heart pain and fever, blockage within with vomiting, corresponding in the same manner as lesser yin. When yang brightness is at the spring: if the guest dominates, clear qi stirs below, with hardness and fullness of the lower abdomen and frequent watery bowel movements; if the host dominates, there is heaviness of the lower back with abdominal pain, cold arising in the lower abdomen giving rise to duck-slop stools below, cold reversal in the intestines surging up into the chest, and in severe cases panting such that one cannot stand for long. When greater yang is at the spring, if cold returns and there is a surplus within, there is pain in the lower back and buttocks with difficulty bending and stretching, and pain in the thighs, shins, feet, and knees.' The Emperor said: 'Good. How does one treat these?' Qibo said: 'What is high, suppress it; what is low, raise it; what is in excess, break it down; what is insufficient, supplement it; assist it with what benefits it, and harmonize it with what suits it. One must settle the host and guest qi, and adapt to the cold or warmth appropriate, where they are alike, treat by contrary means; where they differ, treat by compliant means.' The Emperor said: 'Treating cold with heat and heat with cold, treating by contrary means when the qi accord with each other and by compliant means when they do not, this I already understand. But what of the proper flavors involved?' Qibo said: 'For the host of the wood position, one drains with sourness and supplements with acridity. For the host of the fire position, one drains with sweetness and supplements with saltiness. For the host of the earth position, one drains with bitterness and supplements with sweetness. For the host of the metal position, one drains with acridity and supplements with sourness. For the host of the water position, one drains with saltiness and supplements with bitterness. For the guest qi of reverting yin, supplement with acridity, drain with sourness, and moderate with sweetness. For the guest qi of lesser yin, supplement with saltiness, drain with sweetness, and astringe with saltiness. For the guest qi of greater yin, supplement with sweetness, drain with bitterness, and moderate with sweetness. For the guest qi of lesser yang, supplement with saltiness, drain with sweetness, and soften with saltiness. For the guest qi of yang brightness, supplement with sourness, drain with acridity, and drain further with bitterness. For the guest qi of greater yang, supplement with bitterness, drain with saltiness, firm with bitterness, and moisten with acridity, thereby opening the interstices and pores, bringing about the circulation of fluids and the free flow of qi.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. I wish to hear what is meant by the three divisions of yin and yang.' Qibo said: 'The qi has greater and lesser quantities, hence differing functions.' The Emperor said: 'What is meant by yang brightness?' Qibo said: 'It is where the two yang combine into brightness.' The Emperor said: 'What is reverting yin?' Qibo said: 'It is where the two yin meet and are exhausted.' The Emperor said: 'The qi has greater and lesser quantities, disease has flourishing and waning, treatment has the gradual and the urgent, and formulas have the large and the small, I wish to hear the essential rule governing these.' Qibo said: 'The qi has high and low positions, disease has near and far locations, symptoms have internal and external aspects, and treatment has light and heavy degrees, the aim is to have the treatment reach precisely the site of disease. The Da Yao states: one sovereign ingredient and two ministers constitute an odd-numbered formula; two sovereigns and four ministers constitute an even-numbered formula; two sovereigns and three ministers constitute an odd-numbered formula; two sovereigns and six ministers constitute an even-numbered formula. Hence it is said: for disease nearby, use odd-numbered formulas; for disease far off, use even-numbered formulas. For inducing sweating, do not use odd-numbered formulas; for purgation, do not use even-numbered formulas. To supplement the upper and treat the upper, formulate with gradual action; to supplement the lower and treat the lower, formulate with urgent action. What is urgent has thick qi and flavor; what is gradual has thin qi and flavor, the aim being to have it reach precisely the site. This is what is meant. When the site of disease is distant, and the qi and flavor of the medicine would be absorbed along the way, one should take the medicine with food so that it passes beyond the intervening region, one must not exceed the proper regulation. Therefore, in the way of leveling the qi: for disease nearby, whether odd or even, formulate a small dose; for disease far off, whether odd or even, formulate a large dose. Large doses are taken in fewer administrations; small doses are taken in more administrations, at most, nine times; at least, twice. If an odd-numbered formula fails to resolve the disease, follow with an even-numbered one, this is called a doubled formula. If the even-numbered formula still fails to resolve it, then employ a paradoxical adjuvant to achieve the effect, this is what is meant by cold, heat, warm, and cool agents being used contrary to, yet in accordance with, the nature of the disease.' The Emperor said: 'Good. That disease arises from the root, this I already understand. But when it arises from the branch, how does one treat it?' Qibo said: 'When the disease runs contrary to its root, one obtains a disease of the branch; the treatment then runs contrary to the root, and one obtains a formula that addresses the branch.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. By what signs does one observe the dominance of the six qi?' Qibo said: 'One observes their arrival: when clear qi comes strongly, this is the dominance of dryness; wind-wood receives the pathogenic influence, and disease of the liver is generated. When heat qi comes strongly, this is the dominance of fire; metal-dryness receives the pathogenic influence, and disease of the lung is generated. When cold qi comes strongly, this is the dominance of water; fire-heat receives the pathogenic influence, and disease of the heart is generated. When damp qi comes strongly, this is the dominance of earth; cold-water receives the pathogenic influence, and disease of the kidney is generated. When wind qi comes strongly, this is the dominance of wood; earth-dampness receives the pathogenic influence, and disease of the spleen is generated. This is what is meant by contracting a pathogenic influence and thereby generating disease. If one is exposed at a time when the year's qi is deficient, the pathogenic influence will be severe. If the harmony proper to the season is lost, the pathogenic influence will also be severe. If one encounters the moon's waning emptiness, the pathogenic influence will also be severe. If one is doubly exposed to pathogenic influence, the disease becomes critical. Wherever there is a dominant qi, a return is bound to follow.' The Emperor said: 'How does the pulse arrive in each case?' Qibo said: 'At the arrival of reverting yin, the pulse is wiry; at the arrival of lesser yin, the pulse is hook-like; at the arrival of greater yin, the pulse is deep; at the arrival of lesser yang, the pulse is large and floating; at the arrival of yang brightness, the pulse is short and choppy; at the arrival of greater yang, the pulse is large and long. If it arrives in a balanced manner, this is normal; if it arrives excessively, this is disease; if it arrives in a contrary manner, this is disease; if it fails to arrive when it should, this is disease; if it arrives before it should, this is disease; and if yin and yang are reversed in their expected arrival, the condition is critical.'

The Emperor said: 'The root and branch of the six qi are followed differently, how is this?' Qibo said: 'Among the qi, some follow the root, some follow both root and branch, and some follow neither root nor branch.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear the whole of it.' Qibo said: 'Lesser yang and greater yin follow the root; lesser yin and greater yang follow both the root and the branch; yang brightness and reverting yin follow neither root nor branch, but follow the intermediate paired qi instead. Hence, for those that follow the root, transformation is generated from the root; for those that follow both root and branch, there is transformation of both root and branch; for those that follow the intermediate qi, transformation occurs by means of the intermediate qi.' The Emperor said: 'When the pulse is compliant yet the disease is contrary, how does one diagnose this?' Qibo said: 'The pulse arrives in a seemingly compliant manner, yet upon pressing it does not beat vigorously, this holds true for all the yang conditions.' The Emperor said: 'What of the pulse in the contrariety of the yin conditions?' Qibo said: 'The pulse arrives in a seemingly compliant manner, yet upon pressing it beats vigorously and is full. Hence, in the arising of the hundred diseases, some are generated from the root, some from the branch, some from the intermediate qi; some are resolved by treating the root, some by treating the branch, some by treating the intermediate qi, some by treating both branch and root; some are resolved by contrary treatment, some by compliant treatment. What appears contrary may in truth be compliant; what appears compliant may in truth be contrary. Hence it is said: he who knows branch and root can apply treatment without peril; he who clearly knows contrariety and compliance can act correctly without hesitation. This is what is meant. He who does not know this is unfit to speak of diagnosis, and is apt to throw the classics into confusion. Hence the Da Yao states: the crude practitioner chatters glibly, believing he knows it all; he says the heat has not yet ceased, when in fact a cold disease has already begun anew. Though the qi is the same, its manifestation differs, and he becomes confused in diagnosis and throws the classics into disorder. This is what is meant. Now, the way of branch and root is at once concise and broad, small yet vast, one may speak of a single principle and thereby know the harm of the hundred diseases. To speak of branch and root is easy, provided nothing is lost; to examine root and branch enables the qi to be brought into balance. He who clearly understands dominance and return becomes a model for the myriad people, and the Way of heaven is thereby fulfilled.'

The Emperor said: 'In the changes of dominance and return, how do early and late timing come about?' Qibo said: 'As for the dominant qi: once dominance arrives, disease has already set in; and once the disease has become sullenly established, the return has already begun to sprout. As for the return: it arises once the dominance has run its course, and becomes severe once it attains its proper seasonal position. Dominance may be slight or severe, and return may be little or great; where the dominance is mild, the return is mild; where the dominance is depleted, the return is depleted, this is the constancy of heaven.' The Emperor said: 'When dominance and return occur, their movement sometimes fails to match the proper seasonal position, or arrives later than the expected time, what is the reason for this?' Qibo said: 'The generation of qi differs from the waxing and waning of its transformation. The functioning of the waxing and waning of cold, heat, warmth, and coolness lies in the four intercalary corners. Hence the movement of yang begins with warmth and reaches its height in summer heat; the movement of yin begins with clearness and reaches its height in cold. Spring, summer, autumn, and winter each vary somewhat in their divisions. Hence the Da Yao states: the mildness of spring becomes the heat of summer; the harshness of autumn becomes the fury of winter. Carefully examine the four intercalary corners, and all the signs of observation converge there, their culmination can be seen, and their inception can be known. This is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'Does this variance have a fixed measure?' Qibo said: 'It amounts in all to about thirty degrees.' The Emperor said: 'How does the pulse respond in each case?' Qibo said: 'The variance follows the same regular pattern, awaiting its proper time before departing. The Mai Yao states: in spring the pulse should not be deep; in summer it should not be wiry; in winter it should not be choppy; in autumn it should not be rapid, this is called the four blockages. If deepness is excessive, this is disease; if wiriness is excessive, this is disease; if choppiness is excessive, this is disease; if rapidity is excessive, this is disease. If these signs appear intermingled, this is disease; if they reappear out of season, this is disease; if a sign departs before it should, this is disease; if a sign fails to depart when it should, this is disease; and if the reverse of the seasonal norm occurs, death results. Hence it is said: the mutual custody of the qi between seasons and pulses is like a balance scale that must not lose its equilibrium. As for the qi of yin and yang: when clear and still, generation and transformation proceed in order; when disturbed, grave illness arises. This is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'What of the obscure and the bright?' Qibo said: 'Where the two yin meet and are exhausted, it is called obscure; where the two yang combine into brightness, it is called bright. The pairing of the obscure and the bright corresponds to the distinction between cold and heat.' The Emperor said: 'What of the equinoxes and solstices?' Qibo said: 'The point where the qi culminates is called a solstice; the point where the qi divides is called an equinox. At a solstice, the qi is the same; at an equinox, the qi is differentiated, this is what is called the proper regulation of heaven and earth.' The Emperor said: 'You, Master, have said that the qi of spring and autumn begins beforehand, while the qi of winter and summer begins afterward, this I already understand. Yet the coming and going of the six qi and their governance of the year are not constant, how, then, does one supplement and drain?' Qibo said: 'What is governed above and below, follow what is beneficial to each; rectifying the flavor used is the essential point, the same method applies to left and right. The Da Yao states: for the governance of lesser yang, use sweetness first and saltiness afterward; for yang brightness, use acridity first and sourness afterward; for greater yang, use saltiness first and bitterness afterward; for reverting yin, use sourness first and acridity afterward; for lesser yin, use sweetness first and saltiness afterward; for greater yin, use bitterness first and sweetness afterward. Assist with what is beneficial and nourish with what generates the needed qi, this is called attaining the qi.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. Now, the hundred diseases that arise are all generated from wind, cold, summer-heat, dampness, dryness, and fire, through their transformations and changes. The classics say: what is in excess, drain it; what is deficient, supplement it. I have granted this to the practitioners, yet the practitioners' application of it has still not achieved complete success. I wish to make the essential Way infallible in practice, responding like drumstick to drum, as swift and sure as pulling out a thorn or washing away a stain, the skill of the crafty, the subtle, the spiritlike, and the sagely. May I hear of it?' Qibo said: 'To carefully examine the pivotal mechanism of disease without losing what is proper to the qi, this is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear what the pivotal mechanisms of disease are.' Qibo said: 'All conditions of wind with trembling and dizziness belong to the liver. All conditions of cold with contraction and tension belong to the kidney. All conditions of qi with oppression and stifling belong to the lung. All conditions of dampness with swelling and fullness belong to the spleen. All conditions of heat with clouded vision and convulsion belong to fire. All diseases of itching and sores belong to the heart. All conditions of reversal, retention, and discharge belong to the lower region. All conditions of wilting, panting, and vomiting belong to the upper region. All conditions of chattering teeth and trembling, as though the spirit had lost its guard, belong to fire. All conditions of spasm and stiffness of the nape belong to dampness. All conditions of contrary surging upward belong to fire. All conditions of distension with enlarged abdomen belong to heat. All conditions of agitation, mania, and reckless behavior belong to fire. All conditions of sudden rigidity and stiffness belong to wind. All conditions with sound, such that percussion of the abdomen resounds like a drum, belong to heat. All diseases of swelling, aching pain and soreness, fright and alarm belong to fire. All conditions of twisting, contortion, and turbid bodily fluids belong to heat. All diseases with clear, cold, and limpid bodily fluids belong to cold. All conditions of vomiting of sour fluid and sudden pouring diarrhea with urgent tenesmus belong to heat. Hence the Da Yao states: carefully hold to the pivotal mechanisms of disease, and attend to each according to its category. Where a sign is present, seek its cause; where it is absent, still seek its cause. Where there is excess, hold it accountable; where there is deficiency, hold it accountable. One must first determine the dominance among the five phases, unblock the blood and qi, bring about their smooth flow, and achieve harmony and balance. This is what is meant.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What of the yin-yang functions of the five flavors?' Qibo said: 'Acrid and sweet flavors, which effuse and disperse, are yang; sour and bitter flavors, which surge upward and drain downward, are yin; the salty flavor, which surges upward and drains downward, is yin; the bland flavor, which percolates and drains, is yang. Of these six flavors, some astringe, some disperse, some moderate, some quicken, some dry, some moisten, some soften, some firm, one applies them according to what is beneficial, regulating the qi so as to bring it into balance.' The Emperor said: 'For conditions not resolved by regulating the qi alone, how does one treat them? Among substances toxic and non-toxic, which comes first and which comes after? I wish to hear this principle.' Qibo said: 'Whether toxic or non-toxic, what is treated takes precedence, and the formula is regulated according to the disease's magnitude.' The Emperor said: 'Please tell me of this regulation.' Qibo said: 'One sovereign and two ministers constitute a small formula; one sovereign, three ministers, and five assistants constitute a medium formula; one sovereign, three ministers, and nine assistants constitute a large formula. What is cold, heat it; what is hot, cool it; what is mild, oppose it directly; what is severe, follow it with paradoxical treatment; what is hardened, whittle it away; what is a lodged pathogen, expel it; what is fatigued from taxation, warm it; what is bound, disperse it; what is retained, attack it; what is dry, moisten it; what is urgent, moderate it; what is dispersed, gather it; what is damaged, warm it; what is sluggish, move it; what is startled, calm it. Treat by raising or by lowering, by rubbing or by bathing, by pressing or by forceful seizure, by opening or by venting, in each case, fit the treatment to the matter at hand.'

The Emperor said: 'What is meant by contrary and compliant treatment?' Qibo said: 'Contrary treatment is direct treatment; compliant treatment is paradoxical treatment. Whether one follows it a little or a great deal depends on observing the particular case.' The Emperor said: 'What is meant by paradoxical treatment?' Qibo said: 'Heat medicine is used by means of cold symptoms, cold medicine is used by means of heat symptoms, blocking agents are used for conditions of blockage, and freely flowing agents are used for conditions of free flow. One must subdue the governing pathology, while first addressing its underlying cause. At the outset the treatment and symptom appear alike, but in the end they differ. In this way one may cause accumulations to be broken up, hardness to be dissolved, the qi to be harmonized, and the disease to be surely resolved.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What of cases resolved simply by regulating the qi?' Qibo said: 'Treat by contrariety, or treat by compliance; treat contrarily yet in a compliant manner, or treat compliantly yet in a contrary manner. Unblocking the qi so as to bring it into regulation is the principle.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of disease located internally and externally?' Qibo said: 'When disease proceeds from within to without, regulate the interior; when it proceeds from without to within, treat the exterior. When it proceeds from within to without and becomes predominant in the exterior, first regulate the interior and afterward treat the exterior; when it proceeds from without to within and becomes predominant in the interior, first treat the exterior and afterward regulate the interior. When the interior and exterior are unrelated to each other, treat the governing disease directly.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. When fire-heat is followed by aversion to cold, with fever resembling malaria, occurring sometimes once daily and sometimes once every several days, what is the reason for this?' Qibo said: 'This is because, at the time when the dominant and returning qi meet, there is a difference in their relative abundance. If yin qi is abundant and yang qi scant, the onset occurs at longer intervals; if yang qi is abundant and yin qi scant, the onset occurs at shorter intervals. This is the rhythm of the mutual clash and waxing-waning of dominance and return, malaria follows the same pattern.' The Emperor said: 'The classic texts say to treat cold with heat and heat with cold, and practitioners cannot abandon this rule and alter this Way. Yet there are cases of heat disease that, when treated with cold, remain hot, and cases of cold disease that, when treated with heat, remain cold, in both cases a new disease arises anew. How should these be treated?' Qibo said: 'In all cases where cooling treatment fails to relieve heat, seek the root in yin; where warming treatment fails to relieve cold, seek the root in yang. This is what is meant by seeking its category.' The Emperor said: 'Good. Why is it that taking a cold medicine instead produces heat, and taking a hot medicine instead produces cold?' Qibo said: 'This is because one has treated the already dominant qi, and hence the result is contrary.' The Emperor said: 'What of when this occurs even without treating the dominant qi?' Qibo said: 'How thorough is this question! This is due not to treating the dominant qi, but to the affinities of the five flavors. Now, when the five flavors enter the stomach, each returns to what it favors: sourness enters the liver first, bitterness the heart first, sweetness the spleen first, acridity the lung first, and saltiness the kidney first. Over time, this augments the corresponding qi, this is the constant pattern by which substances transform the body. Yet if the augmentation of qi persists too long, this becomes the cause of premature death.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What is meant by sovereign and minister in the composition of a formula?' Qibo said: 'That which governs the disease is called the sovereign; that which assists the sovereign is called the minister; that which corresponds to the minister is called the envoy, this is not the same as the three grades of superior, middle, and inferior in classifying materia medica.' The Emperor said: 'What are the three grades?' Qibo said: 'They serve to clarify the distinct categories of the beneficial and the harmful.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of disease internal and external?' Qibo said: 'In the method of regulating the qi, one must distinguish yin and yang, determine what is internal and external, and let each keep to its own domain. What is internal, treat internally; what is external, treat externally. What is mild, regulate it; what is more than mild, level it; what is exuberant, seize it. For excess that would be treated by sweating, instead purge it when appropriate. Cold, heat, warmth, and coolness are each diminished according to their category. Follow what is beneficial, adhere carefully to the Way as prescribed, and every action will attain complete success; the qi and blood will be rightly balanced, and one will long preserve the mandate of heaven.' The Emperor said: 'Good.'
